questions.question: Explain the construction of a coaxial cable and mention its two common types. Also list two advantages and typical applications in networking.
keyPoints: Construction: central copper conductor, insulating dielectric, braided metal shield, outer plastic jacket.
Advantages: excellent shielding against EMI, higher bandwidth than twisted pair.
Typical applications: legacy Ethernet (10BASE2), cable television distribution, CCTV systems.

questions.question: Differentiate between single‑mode and multimode fiber‑optic cables with respect to core diameter, modal dispersion, bandwidth/distance capability, and typical use cases.
keyPoints: Core diameter: single‑mode ≈8‑10 µm, multimode ≈50‑62.5 µm.
Modal dispersion: single‑mode virtually none, multimode significant.
Bandwidth & distance: single‑mode supports tens of km at 10 Gbps+; multimode limited to ≤300 m at comparable speeds.
Typical uses: single‑mode for long‑haul telecom and WAN backbones; multimode for LANs, data‑center interconnects, and short‑reach campus networks.

questions.question: Describe in detail the three main types of guided transmission media used in computer networks: twisted‑pair, coaxial, and fiber‑optic. Discuss their construction, categories/grades, performance characteristics, advantages, disadvantages, and typical applications.
keyPoints: Twisted‑pair: pairs of copper wires twisted to reduce EMI; categories (Cat5e, Cat6, Cat7), UTP/STP, used for Ethernet LANs, telephone wiring.
Coaxial: central conductor, dielectric, shield, jacket; types (thick‑coax, thin‑coax), better shielding and bandwidth than twisted‑pair; historically used for 10 BASE‑2 Ethernet and cable TV.

Fiber‑optic: glass or plastic core/cladding, total internal reflection; multimode (50/62.5 µm) and single‑mode (≈9 µm) variants; extremely high bandwidth, low attenuation, immune to EMI; employed in backbone networks, high‑speed LANs, WANs, and long‑distance links.

Comparison: twisted‑pair is cheap and easy but limited distance/bandwidth; coaxial offers higher shielding but is bulkier; fiber provides highest speed/distance at higher cost and requires skilled termination.

questions.question: Explain unguided (wireless) transmission media, covering radio waves, microwaves, infrared, and satellite communication. Include frequency ranges, propagation characteristics, advantages, limitations, and practical examples of their use in computer networks.
keyPoints: Radio waves: 3 kHz‑300 GHz, omnidirectional, can penetrate walls; used in Wi‑Fi (2.4/5 GHz), Bluetooth, cellular networks, RFID.
Microwaves: 300 MHz‑300 GHz, line‑of‑sight, high bandwidth, point‑to‑point links; terrestrial microwave backhauls and point‑to‑point data links.
Infrared: 300 GHz‑400 THz, line‑of‑sight, short range, low interference; used in TV remotes, IrDA data transfer, short‑distance LANs.
Satellite: frequencies in C (4‑8 GHz), Ku (12‑18 GHz), Ka (26‑40 GHz) bands; provides wide‑area coverage; applications include broadband Internet, VSAT networks, GPS, and broadcast TV.
